mnemonics.title: Signal Types Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: CT signals: Continuous Time = Continuous Value range; DT signals: Discrete Time = Numbered samples only. Think 'CT = Continuous, DT = Digital/Discrete'.

mnemonics.title: Even-Odd Decomposition
mnemonics.content: Even part: (x(t) + x(-t))/2; Odd part: (x(t) - x(-t))/2. Remember: Even = add both, divide by 2; Odd = subtract, divide by 2.

mnemonics.title: Unit Step and Delta Relationship
mnemonics.content: δ(t) = du(t)/dt (derivative of step) and u(t) = ∫δ(τ)dτ (integral of delta). Delta is the 'instant' change, step is the 'accumulation'.

acronyms.usage: A system that is both linear (satisfies superposition) and time-invariant (behavior doesn't change with time). Most DSP systems are modeled as LTI.
Stability criterion: If input is bounded, output remains bounded. For LTI systems, requires absolutely integrable impulse response.
Processing signals using digital techniques, involves sampling, quantization, and computational algorithms.
FT decomposes aperiodic signals into continuous frequencies; FS represents periodic signals as sum of sinusoids.

visualTips.visualization: Draw as horizontal line at 0 for t<0, then jumps to 1 at t=0, stays at 1 for t>0. Like a light switch turning ON at t=0.
Draw as arrow at t=0 with height 1 (or area 1). Some show with value '∞' at t=0. Think of it as an instantaneous spike.
Even: symmetric about Y-axis (mirror left-right same). Odd: anti-symmetric (mirror gives negative, like rotation 180°).
Think 'sliding dot product': flip one signal, slide across the other, multiply overlapping areas, sum products. Visual animation shows overlapping regions.

analogies.analogy: Signals are like letters - they carry information. Continuous signals are like handwritten letters (smooth writing), discrete signals are like typed messages (individual characters).
An LTI system is like a machine that transforms raw materials (input signal) into products (output signal) following fixed rules (impulse response).
Convolution is like mixing two recipes: flip one ingredient list, then combine with the other by adding corresponding items - order matters but final mixture depends on both.
Impulse response h(t) is like DNA or fingerprint of a system - it uniquely identifies how the system will respond to any input through convolution.
